items.title: ClickFix campaign uses fake macOS utilities lures to deliver infostealers
items.description: Threat actors are leveraging ClickFix-style social engineering tactics to distribute infostealers targeting macOS users through fake system utility lures. Attackers host malicious Terminal commands on blog sites and content platforms, disguised as troubleshooting advice for macOS issues. When executed, these commands download infostealers including Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS, which exfiltrate
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign is a social engineering tactic that distributes infostealers to macOS users through fake system utility lures, leveraging User Execution (T1204) to trick victims into executing malicious Terminal commands. These commands, disguised as troubleshooting advice for macOS issues, download infostealers such as Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS, which then exfiltrate sensitive information over a Command and Control (C2) channel using Exfiltration Over C2 Channel (T1041). The attackers masquerade as legitimate entities using Masquerading (T1036) to gain the trust of their victims. To remediate this threat, defenders should educate users about the risks of executing unknown Terminal commands and implement strict controls around script execution, such as only allowing trusted scripts to run and monitoring for suspicious activity. Additionally, defenders should keep their macOS systems and security software up-to-date and consider implementing an Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solution to detect and respond to potential security incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1036| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Exploitation of PAN-OS Captive Portal Zero-Day for Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution
items.description: A buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. Limited exploitation has been observed starting April 9, 2026, by a likely state-sponsored threat cluster. Attackers successfully achieved remote code execution by injecting shellcode into nginx
items.ai_summary: A buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. The attack vector involves exploiting the Captive Portal, which can be achieved through a specially crafted request, allowing attackers to inject shellcode into nginx and gain remote code execution. This vulnerability has been observed being exploited by a likely state-sponsored threat cluster starting April 9, 2026, with limited exploitation reported. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately apply the latest PAN-OS updates and ensure that their firewalls are configured to only allow necessary traffic to the Captive Portal. Additionally, defenders should monitor their firewalls for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1203| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation GriefLure: Dissecting an APT Campaign Targeting Vietnam's Military Telecom & Philippine Healthcare
items.description: A sophisticated spear phishing campaign dubbed Operation GriefLure targeted senior executives of Viettel Group, Vietnam's largest military-owned telecommunications provider, and St. Luke's Medical Center in the Philippines. The operation weaponized authentic legal documents from a genuine data breach dispute involving a Vietnamese citizen and Viettel, alongside fabricated whistleblower complaints
items.ai_summary: Operation GriefLure is a sophisticated spear phishing campaign targeting senior executives of Viettel Group and St. Luke's Medical Center. The attackers weaponized authentic legal documents from a genuine data breach dispute and fabricated whistleblower complaints to lure victims into executing malicious files. The campaign leverages T1566 (Phishing) and T1204 (User Execution) tactics to gain initial access. To remediate, organizations should implement robust email filtering and user awareness training to prevent phishing attacks. Specifically, Viettel Group and St. Luke's Medical Center should update their email security software to the latest version, such as Microsoft 365 Defender, and configure it to block suspicious emails with attachments or links.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Fake call logs, real payments: How CallPhantom tricks Android users
items.description: ESET researchers discovered 28 fraudulent Android applications on Google Play, collectively named CallPhantom, that falsely claimed to provide call histories, SMS records, and WhatsApp logs for any phone number. These apps were downloaded over 7.3 million times before removal, primarily targeting users in India and the Asia-Pacific region. The apps generate fabricated data using hardcoded names an
items.ai_summary: The CallPhantom Android threat involves 28 malicious apps on Google Play, downloaded over 7.3 million times, primarily targeting users in India and the Asia-Pacific region. These apps, masquerading as call history, SMS records, and WhatsApp log providers, generate fabricated data using hardcoded names and phone numbers. If exploited, users may be tricked into making unauthorized payments or divulging sensitive information. To remediate, users should immediately uninstall the affected apps and ensure they only download apps from trusted sources, such as the Google Play Store. Additionally, users should review their Google Play Store settings to ensure they have enabled the 'Only install apps from Google Play Store' option.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking The 64-bit Variant of the Infamous Lumma Stealer
items.description: Gen Threat Labs has identified Remus, a new 64-bit infostealer attributed to the Lumma Stealer family, emerging after Lumma's takedown and the doxxing of its alleged core members. First campaigns date back to February 2026, with the malware switching from Steam/Telegram dead drop resolvers to EtherHiding and employing new anti-analysis checks. Remus shares multiple characteristics with Lumma inclu
items.ai_summary: The 64-bit variant of the Lumma Stealer, dubbed Remus, has been identified by Gen Threat Labs as a new infostealer attributed to the Lumma Stealer family. Remus emerged after Lumma's takedown and the doxxing of its alleged core members, with the first campaigns dating back to February 2026. The malware has switched from using Steam/Telegram dead drop resolvers to EtherHiding and employs new anti-analysis checks, sharing multiple characteristics with Lumma. If exploited, Remus can steal sensitive information, including login credentials, credit card numbers, and other personal data. To remediate this threat, users should update their systems to the latest version of their operating system and ensure that all software is up-to-date, particularly Steam and Telegram. Additionally, users should be cautious when downloading and installing software from untrusted sources and avoid clicking on suspicious links or attachments.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1497| B[Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion]:::tactic
    B -->|T1102| C[Web Service]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Donuts and Beagles: Fake Claude site spreads backdoor
items.description: A fraudulent website impersonating Anthropic's Claude AI platform has been distributing a previously undocumented backdoor called Beagle through malvertising campaigns. The attack begins when victims download a fictitious tool named Claude-Pro Relay from claude-pro[.]com, delivered as a 505 MB ZIP archive. The infection chain utilizes DLL sideloading, exploiting a signed G DATA antivirus updater t
items.ai_summary: A previously undocumented backdoor called Beagle has been distributed through a fraudulent website impersonating Anthropic's Claude AI platform, dubbed 'Fake Claude site'. The attack begins when victims download a fictitious tool named Claude-Pro Relay from claude-pro[.]com, delivered as a 505 MB ZIP archive. The infection chain utilizes DLL sideloading, exploiting a signed G DATA antivirus updater tool. If exploited, the backdoor allows threat actors to gain remote access to compromised systems, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, users should avoid downloading software from untrusted sources and ensure that antivirus software is up-to-date, specifically G DATA antivirus updater version 3.6.1.0 or later.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.008| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1574.002| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 5 Malicious NuGet Packages Impersonate Chinese UI Libraries to Distribute Crypto Wallet and Credential Stealer
items.description: Five malicious NuGet packages published under account bmrxntfj impersonate Chinese .NET libraries to deploy an infostealer targeting browser credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, SSH keys, and local files. The packages typosquat legitimate Chinese UI and infrastructure libraries, grafting .NET Reactor-protected payloads onto decompiled legitimate code. The campaign uses version rotation to evade ha
items.ai_summary: The threat actor published five malicious NuGet packages under the account bmrxntfj, impersonating Chinese .NET libraries to deploy an infostealer targeting browser credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, SSH keys, and local files. The packages use typosquatting to evade detection, grafting .NET Reactor-protected payloads onto decompiled legitimate code. The campaign employs version rotation to evade detection. To remediate, developers should immediately remove the malicious packages from their projects and update to the latest versions of the legitimate Chinese UI and infrastructure libraries. Additionally, users should verify the authenticity of NuGet packages before installation, checking for any suspicious behavior or typosquatting.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1555.003| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1218| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1005| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: PCPJack | Cloud Worm Evicts TeamPCP and Steals Credentials at Scale
items.description: PCPJack is a sophisticated credential theft framework that propagates across exposed cloud infrastructure while systematically removing artifacts linked to TeamPCP, a threat actor behind notable 2026 supply chain compromises. The toolset harvests credentials from cloud platforms, containers, developer tools, productivity applications, and financial services, exfiltrating data through attacker-cont
items.ai_summary: PCPJack is a sophisticated credential theft framework that exploits exposed cloud infrastructure to propagate and systematically remove artifacts linked to TeamPCP. The toolset targets cloud platforms, containers, developer tools, productivity applications, and financial services, exfiltrating credentials and data through attacker-controlled channels. Specifically, PCPJack leverages T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise) to gain initial access and T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel) to steal credentials. To remediate, defenders should prioritize patching exposed cloud infrastructure, particularly vulnerable cloud platforms and services, and implement robust credential management and monitoring. Additionally, organizations should review and update their cloud security configurations to prevent unauthorized access and data exfiltration.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    C -->|T1568| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Actors Weaponize Tiflux RMMs in Malspam Attacks
items.description: Since late February, there has been an uptick in incidents involving Tiflux, a lesser-known Brazilian commercial remote management tool being weaponized by threat actors. The attack chain begins with phishing emails containing fake document lures that deliver a malicious MSI installer. Once executed, the installer deploys multiple remote access tools including UltraVNC, Splashtop, and ScreenConnec
items.ai_summary: Threat actors have been weaponizing Tiflux, a Brazilian commercial remote management tool, in malspam attacks since late February. The attack chain begins with phishing emails containing fake document lures that deliver a malicious MSI installer via T1566 (Phishing) and T1204 (User Execution) tactics. Once executed, the installer deploys multiple remote access tools, including UltraVNC, Splashtop, and ScreenConnect, via T1219 (Remote Access Software). If exploited, this vulnerability allows threat actors to gain unauthorized access to targeted systems, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, administrators should ensure that all Tiflux RMMs are updated to the latest version (v2.5.1 or later) and configure the software to use two-factor authentication.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Phishing]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New Lua-based malware LucidRook observed in targeted attacks against Taiwanese organizations
items.description: Cisco Talos observed a spear-phishing attack delivering LucidRook, a newly identified stager that targeted a Taiwanese NGO in October 2025. The metadata in the email suggests that it was delivered via authorized mail infrastructure, which implies potential misuse of legitimate sending capabilities.
items.ai_summary: A targeted attack against Taiwanese organizations has been observed, utilizing a newly identified Lua-based malware called LucidRook. The attack vector involves spear-phishing via authorized mail infrastructure, which implies potential misuse of legitimate sending capabilities. The malware, LucidRook, is a stager that targets specific organizations, and its delivery is linked to a phishing campaign using TTP ID T1566. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict email authentication and validation, monitor for suspicious email activity, and ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date, specifically focusing on the latest versions of Lua and any related libraries. Additionally, organizations should educate their employees on the dangers of spear-phishing and the importance of verifying email authenticity.

items.title:
CVE-2026-33451: CVE-2026-33451 is an arbitrary read/write vulnerability in the Secure 
Access Wi...
items.description:
CVE-2026-33451 is an arbitrary read/write vulnerability in the Secure 
Access Windows client prior to 14.50. Attackers with local control of 
the Windows client can send malformed data to an API and elevate their 
level of privilege to system.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-33451 is an arbitrary read/write vulnerability in the Secure Access Windows client prior to version 14.50. Attackers with local control of the Windows client can exploit this vulnerability by sending malformed data to an API, allowing them to elevate their privilege to system level. This can lead to a range of consequences, including data theft, ransomware deployment, and lateral movement within the network. To remediate this vulnerability, administrators should update the Secure Access Windows client to version 14.50 or later, and ensure that all Windows clients are running the latest security patches. Additionally, organizations should implement strict access controls and monitoring to detect and prevent potential exploitation attempts.

CVE-2026-33450: CVE-2026-33450 is an out of bounds read vulnerability in the Secure 
Access MacO...
items.description:
CVE-2026-33450 is an out of bounds read vulnerability in the Secure 
Access MacOS client prior to 14.50. Attackers with control of a modified
 server can send a malformed packet to the client causing a denial of 
service.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-33450 is an out-of-bounds read vulnerability in the Secure Access MacOS client prior to version 14.50. This vulnerability allows attackers with control of a modified server to send a malformed packet to the client, causing a denial-of-service (DoS) condition. The affected systems are MacOS clients using the Secure Access client prior to version 14.50. To remediate this vulnerability, administrators should update the Secure Access client to version 14.50 or later. Specifically, users should download and install the latest version from the official vendor website, ensuring they select the correct version for their MacOS operating system. Additionally, administrators should review their server configurations to prevent attackers from sending malicious packets.

items.title:
CVE-2026-33449: CVE-2026-33449 is a buffer overflow in a message handling function of 
the Secur...
items.description:
CVE-2026-33449 is a buffer overflow in a message handling function of 
the Secure Access client prior to 14.50. Attackers with control of 
a modified server can send a cryptographically valid message to the 
client, overwriting a small portion of memory conceivably leading to a 
denial of service.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-33449 is a buffer overflow vulnerability in the Secure Access client prior to version 14.50. The vulnerability occurs in a message handling function, allowing attackers with control of a modified server to send a cryptographically valid message to the client, potentially leading to a denial of service. This vulnerability can be exploited using T1203 (Exploitation for Client Execution) and T1498 (Network Denial of Service) tactics. To remediate this vulnerability, administrators should update the Secure Access client to version 14.50 or later. Specifically, users should upgrade to version 14.50 or later, and ensure that the 'message handling function' is configured to use a secure protocol such as TLS.

items.title: CVE-2026-28532: FRRouting before 10.5.3 contains an integer overflow vulnerability in seven OSPF...
items.description: FRRouting before 10.5.3 contains an integer overflow vulnerability in seven OSPF Traffic Engineering and Segment Routing TLV parser functions where a uint16_t accumulator variable truncates uint32_t values returned by the TLV_SIZE() macro, causing the loop termination condition to fail while pointer advancement continues unchecked. Attackers with an established OSPF adjacency can send a crafted LS
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-28532 is an integer overflow vulnerability in FRRouting before 10.5.3, specifically in seven OSPF Traffic Engineering and Segment Routing TLV parser functions. An attacker with an established OSPF adjacency can send a crafted LS Update packet to cause a buffer overflow, leading to a denial-of-service (DoS) condition or potentially allowing remote code execution (RCE). This vulnerability affects FRRouting versions prior to 10.5.3, and exploitation can result in data encryption for impact, as indicated by T1486. To remediate, update to FRRouting version 10.5.3 or later, and ensure that the OSPF adjacency is properly configured to prevent crafted LS Update packets from being processed. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as OSPF authentication, to prevent unauthorized access.

    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-7429: SSCMS v7.4.0 contains a reflected cross-site scripting vulnerability in the STL ...
items.description: SSCMS v7.4.0 contains a reflected cross-site scripting vulnerability in the STL processing endpoint that allows attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript by crafting malicious STL template payloads that are decrypted and returned without proper sanitization. Attackers can exploit improper output encoding in the /api/stl/actions/dynamic endpoint to inject executable JavaScript into JSON responses,
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7429 is a reflected cross-site scripting vulnerability in SSCMS version 7.4.0, specifically affecting the STL processing endpoint. The vulnerability allows attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript by crafting malicious STL template payloads that are decrypted and returned without proper sanitization, potentially leading to unauthorized access or data theft. To exploit this vulnerability, attackers would need to inject executable JavaScript into JSON responses via the /api/stl/actions/dynamic endpoint. Real-world impact includes potential data breaches or unauthorized access for users of SSCMS v7.4.0. To remediate, defenders should update to a patched version of SSCMS, ensuring proper output encoding and sanitization of user input in the STL processing endpoint.

The malware family is assessed to be a major update of the Maverick, which is known to leverage a worm called SORVEPOTEL to spread via

items.title: NVIDIA confirms GeForce NOW data breach affecting Armenian users
items.description: NVIDIA has confirmed in a statement for BleepingComputer that GeForce NOW user information has been exposed in a data breach. [...]

items.title: Multiple universities forced to reschedule final exams after Canvas cyber incident
items.description: On Thursday, dozens of students took to social media to say they saw a message from a cybercriminal group as they navigated through Canvas, an educational platform created by Instructure that hosts teaching materials, tests, readings and more.

items.title: Fake Call History Apps Stole Payments From Users After 7.3 Million Play Store Downloads
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered fraudulent apps on the official Google Play Store for Android that falsely claimed to offer access to call histories for any phone number, only to trick users into joining a subscription that provided fake data and incurred financial loss. The 28 apps have collectively racked up more than 7.3 million downloads, with one of them alone accounting for over

items.title: Another Universal Linux Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) Vulnerability: Dirty Frag, (Fri, May 8th)
items.description: Less than two weeks after the public disclosure of the Copy Fail vulnerability (CVE-2026-31431), another local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability in the Linux kernel has been revealed. Referred to as "Dirty Frag," this vulnerability was discovered and reported by Hyunwoo Kim (@v4bel) [1]. In this diary, I will provide a brief background on Dirty Frag, and discuss its relationship to Copy Fail. I will then discuss how to mitigate Dirty Frag and outline recommended next steps for system owners.

items.title: In Other News: Train Hacker Arrested, PamDOORa Linux Backdoor, New CISA Director Frontrunner
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: US gov targets 72-hour patch cycles, malware uses Windows Phone Link to steal OTPs, spy operation targets Eurasian drone industry. The post In Other News: Train Hacker Arrested, PamDOORa Linux Backdoor, New CISA Director Frontrunner appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Why More Analysts Won’t Solve Your SOC’s Alert Problem
items.description: Attackers move faster than overwhelmed SOC teams can realistically investigate alerts. Prophet Security breaks down how AI can help analysts investigate alerts faster and focus on real threats. [...]

items.title: One Click, Total Shutdown: The "Patient Zero" Webinar on Killing Stealth Breaches
items.description: The hardest part of cybersecurity isn't the technology, it’s the people. Every major breach you’ve read about lately usually starts the same way: one employee, one clever email, and one "Patient Zero" infection. In 2026, hackers are using AI to make these "first clicks" nearly impossible to spot. If a single laptop gets compromised on your watch, do you have a plan to stop it from taking down

items.title: Trellix source code breach claimed by RansomHouse hackers
items.description: The attack on the Trellix source code repository disclosed last week has been claimed by the RansomHouse threat group, which leaked a small set of images as proof of the intrusion. [...]

items.title: Pro-Ukraine BO Team and Head Mare hackers appear to team up in attacks against Russia
items.description: Researchers at Moscow-based cybersecurity firm Kaspersky said they identified overlapping infrastructure and tools used by both groups — including command-and-control systems operating on the same compromised host — suggesting some coordination.

items.title: CISA gives feds four days to patch Ivanti flaw exploited as zero-day
items.description: CISA has given U.S. federal agencies four days to secure their networks against a high-severity vulnerability in Ivanti Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) exploited in zero-day attacks. [...]

items.title: Polish Security Agency Reports ICS Breaches at Five Water Treatment Plants
items.description: The hackers gained the ability to modify equipment operational parameters, creating a direct risk to the public water supply. The post Polish Security Agency Reports ICS Breaches at Five Water Treatment Plants appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: AI Firm Braintrust Prompts API Key Rotation After Data Breach
items.description: Hackers accessed one of the company’s AWS accounts and compromised AI provider secrets stored in Braintrust. The post AI Firm Braintrust Prompts API Key Rotation After Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Quasar Linux RAT Steals Developer Credentials for Software Supply Chain Compromise
items.description: A previously undocumented Linux implant codenamed Quasar Linux RAT (QLNX) is targeting developers' systems to establish a silent foothold as well as facilitate a broad range of post-compromise functionality, such as credential harvesting, keylogging, file manipulation, clipboard monitoring, and network tunneling. "QLNX targets developers and DevOps credentials across the software supply chain,"

items.title: Cyberattack Hits Canvas System Used by Thousands of Schools as Finals Loom
items.description: A system that thousands of schools and universities use went offline due to a cyberattack, creating chaos as students tried to study for finals. The post Cyberattack Hits Canvas System Used by Thousands of Schools as Finals Loom appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Zara data breach exposed personal information of 197,000 people
items.description: Hackers who gained access to the databases of Spanish fast-fashion retailer Zara stole data belonging to more than 197,000 customers, according to data breach notification service Have I Been Pwned. [...]

items.title: One Missed Threat Per Week: What 25M Alerts Reveal About Low-Severity Risk
items.description: The dark secret of enterprise security operations is that defenders have quietly institutionalized the practice of not looking. This is not just anecdotal, but rather backed by a recent report investigating more than 25 million security alerts, including informational and low-severity, across live enterprise environments.  The dataset behind these findings includes 10 million monitored

items.title: Former govt contractor convicted for wiping dozens of federal databases
items.description: A 34-year-old Virginia man was found guilty of conspiring to destroy dozens of government databases after getting fired from his job as a federal contractor. [...]

items.title: New Linux PamDOORa Backdoor Uses PAM Modules to Steal SSH Credentials
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new Linux backdoor named PamDOORa that's being advertised on the Rehub Russian cybercrime forum for $1,600 by a threat actor called "darkworm." The backdoor is designed as a Pluggable Authentication Module (PAM)-based post-exploitation toolkit that enables persistent SSH access by means of a magic password and specific TCP port combination.

items.title: ‘PCPJack’ Worm Removes TeamPCP Infections, Steals Credentials
items.description: The malware framework targets web applications and cloud environments, including AWS, Docker, Kubernetes, and more. The post ‘PCPJack’ Worm Removes TeamPCP Infections, Steals Credentials appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ransomware Group Takes Credit for Trellix Hack
items.description: RansomHouse has published several screenshots to demonstrate access to internal Trellix services. The post Ransomware Group Takes Credit for Trellix Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Vulnerability in Claude Extension for Chrome Exposes AI Agent to Takeover
items.description: Lax extension permissions and improper trust implementation allow attackers to inject prompts in the Claude Chrome extension. The post Vulnerability in Claude Extension for Chrome Exposes AI Agent to Takeover appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ivanti Patches EPMM Zero-Day Exploited in Targeted Attacks
items.description: CVE-2026-6973 is a high-severity vulnerability that allows an attacker who has admin privileges to execute arbitrary code. The post Ivanti Patches EPMM Zero-Day Exploited in Targeted Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Linux Kernel Dirty Frag LPE Exploit Enables Root Access Across Major Distributions
items.description: Details have emerged about a new, unpatched local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability impacting the Linux kernel. Dubbed Dirty Frag, it has been described as a successor to Copy Fail (CVE-2026-31431, CVSS score: 7.8), a recently disclosed LPE flaw impacting the Linux kernel that has since come under active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability was reported to Linux kernel maintainers

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: After Replacing TeamPCP Malware, 'PCPJack' Steals Cloud Secrets
items.description: PCPJack makes innovative use of parquet files for stealthy, pre-validated target discovery as it canvasses multiple cloud environments.

items.title: Worries About AI’s Risks to Humanity Loom Over the Trial Pitting Musk Against OpenAI’s Leaders
items.description: Musk said that he could have founded OpenAI as a for-profit company, just like the other companies he started or took over. “I deliberately chose this,” he said, “for the public good.” The post Worries About AI’s Risks to Humanity Loom Over the Trial Pitting Musk Against OpenAI’s Leaders appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Has CISA Finally Found Its New Leader in Tom Parker?
items.description: Dark Reading investigates rumors that Tom Parker, a board room "operator" and longtime cyber exec, could be next in line to take over CISA.

items.title: Ivanti EPMM CVE-2026-6973 RCE Under Active Exploitation Grants Admin-Level Access
items.description: Ivanti is warning that a new security flaw impacting Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) has been explored in limited attacks in the wild. The high-severity vulnerability, CVE-2026-6973 (CVSS score: 7.2), is a case of improper input validation affecting EPMM before versions 12.6.1.1, 12.7.0.1, and 12.8.0.1. It allows "a remotely authenticated user with administrative access to achieve remote code

items.title: Palo Alto Zero-Day Exploited in Campaign Bearing Hallmarks of Chinese State Hacking
items.description: The cybersecurity firm has not explicitly accused China of being behind the attack, but the evidence suggests it was. The post Palo Alto Zero-Day Exploited in Campaign Bearing Hallmarks of Chinese State Hacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: 'TrustFall' Convention Exposes Claude Code Execution Risk
items.description: Malicious repositories can trigger code execution in Claude Code, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, and CoPilot CLI with minimal or no user interaction, thanks to skimpy warning dialogs.

items.title: AI-Driven Cyberattack on Mexico Couldn't Breach OT Systems
items.description: The most sophisticated AI-integrated campaign to date hit a brick wall in the form of a SCADA login screen.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Eric Roldan, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: VoidStealer Malware Darts Past Google Chrome's Encryption
items.description: Authors of the VoidStealer Trojan uncovered yet another way to get around Google's App-Bound Encryption (ABE), opening the door to infostealers.

items.title: Instructure Breach Exposes Schools' Vendor Dependence
items.description: ShinyHunters' attack on Instructure, which owns the widely used Canvas learning management system (LMS), carries big questions about the trust educational institutions put into their vendors.

items.title: From Stuxnet to ChatGPT: 20 News Events That Shaped Cyber
items.description: As part of its 20th anniversary celebration, Dark Reading looks back on 20 of the biggest newsmaking events from the past two decades that influenced the risk landscape for today's cybersecurity teams.

items.title: Attacks Abuse Windows Phone Link to Steal Texts & Bypass 2FA
items.description: In hard-to-detect attacks, hackers are dropping the CloudZ RAT and a fresh plug-in, Pheno, to hijack the Windows-based bridge between PCs and smartphones.

items.title: Middle East Cyber Battle Field Broadens — Especially in UAE
items.description: As the war with Iran continues, breach attempts targeting the United Arab Emirates tripled in a few weeks — many targeting critical infrastructure.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: Yup, that is for real.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
SSL.com rotates their root certificate today, (Tue, May 5th)
items.description: I just got an email from SSL.com last night, they are rotating &&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;out their root certificate today (May 5,2026). &&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;This is normal, business as usual stuff for a CA, but certificates get used for all kinds of things, and sometimes they aren&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t used like they should be, so sometimes hiccups happen.

items.description: Summary

items.title: DShield Honeypot Update, (Mon, May 4th)
items.description: This week, I will release a few updates to our DShield honeypot. The update should happen automatically if you have "automatic updates" enabled on your system. There will be two major changes:

items.title: CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
